title: State of Mobile Dev 2025 Autumn from the Mobius Program Committee
speakers: Andrey Dmitriev, Yuri Dubovoy, Kirill Klebanov
abstract:
In the spring we launched TechRadar to study the mobile development market and find out which tools and approaches you prefer. At the conference opening we will share the results of that research with you.

We will also tell you about the schedule, sessions, and other details. Come to the hall or join online to find out what awaits you in the near future!

title: Wave architecture in practice
abstract:
Last season we saw the Dragon of Complexity that lives in state machines. The Dragon is neither evil nor good, it is part of the nature of the world, and it is useless to fight it. The weak will be devoured by complexity, and the strong, in case of victory, will themselves become a Dragon. This happens again and again: OOP, FP, DDD, any approaches and frameworks created to fight complexity eventually accrue their own — instrumental complexity, no less than the one they tried to handle.

We instinctively try to tame and reduce the Dragon with the help of the law of large numbers, deliberately simplifying the domain model and the user behavior model at the cost of statistics. This strips away the diversity of features, behavior scenarios, and interfaces. In our services only the mathematically optimal — that is, the average, sterile — survives. As a result we find ourselves in a world of monstrous superapps that are surprisingly similar to each other. And only 10–15 years ago mobile development was a bubbling cauldron of diversity!

This sameness and similarity is dangerous. If a Black Swan flies into such a degenerate ecosystem, it dies whole, because it has too few species and in changed conditions there is simply no one to evolve.

Today we can fix all of this. The wave model is capable of changing our approach to work and giving a solid foundation for personalizing services. We will not fight the Dragon of Complexity, we will not tame it — we will go around the Dragon over a wave bridge. In the talk I will show how to build such a bridge in practice.

title: AntiSOLID
abstract:
Everyone has known the SOLID principles since the cradle. They are supposed to serve to improve our code, but what if I told you that blindly following these principles can kill your code?

Let's look at the flip side of SOLID — the AntiSOLID concept. It is a set of antiprinciples that will help you write living, maintainable, and sane code. The concept was originally invented for C#, but in this talk we will examine all the antiprinciples as applied to mobile development.

title: kotlinx.fuzz: Why Fuzz Testing Matters
abstract: We will discuss what Fuzz Testing is, what bugs it has been used to find in the real world, and how the kotlinx.fuzz library — released publicly on April 4, 2025 — helps with them.

title: A new era of mobile development: running LLMs locally on the phone
abstract: We live in a truly amazing new era of large language models. They are becoming more and more efficient, accessible, and close to the average person thanks to model compression and the spread of phones with sufficient computing power. I will tell native and cross-platform developers why local LLMs are a trend of the very near future, how you can already cram intelligence into an app on different technologies, and what it can do.

title: Crash Reporting 101
abstract:
I will tell you how we built a crash reporting service for mobile applications on top of our observability platform Sage. Where the need for a separate service came from and why the solution that worked well for server applications was not enough. We will look at which open source components can be used to implement an analogous service in your own setup, and how, using our experience, to make such a service stable and not a source of headaches.

I will explain why we chose the Sentry SDK, how we learned to group errors, deobfuscate stack traces, and compute correct statistics. Why processing iOS is harder and more expensive, and what additional problems we had to solve for that platform. At the end I will tell you why server applications eventually (unexpectedly) started connecting to us as well, where we decided to grow the service, and how we look at OpenTelemetry.

The talk will be useful for developers who work on mobile application reliability and want to look "under the hood" of crash reporting services. And for engineers responsible for monitoring systems in companies that develop mobile applications.

title: Using AI in development processes
abstract: We will tell you how to use AI in development processes.

title: Camera in KasperskyOS Mobile
abstract:
The research project of a mobile operating system based on KasperskyOS continues to actively develop and grow new services. KasperskyOS is not yet another distribution of Linux or AOSP. It is a microkernel operating system in which all processes are isolated and the interactions between them are strictly controlled by a special security module.

In order to develop applications for an OS with a less than mainstream design, you have to understand not only the structure of the operating system itself but also the architecture of the hardware well — in this case the camera stack on a mobile device.

title: N techniques that will improve the work of the video feed
abstract:
I will tell you how short-video feeds at Yandex are arranged "under the hood," what optimizations and tricks are used, and what we measure in order to improve. It will primarily be of interest to Android developers: you will pick up specific techniques aimed at improving video work and will be able to apply them in your own apps that work with video. iOS and other platform developers will also be able to adopt the experience, since the ideas themselves are cross-platform — and in production we apply them on Android, iOS, and Web.

Deep knowledge of any specific video streaming standard is not expected from you. It will be interesting to listen even if you have no experience with video at all — at least to broaden your horizons and gain an idea of which problems your colleagues solve.

title: Customizing the painter via CustomPainter
Face filters under the hood: Vision, segmentation, and shaders in action
abstract:
Usually an iOS developer writes screens and works with APIs, but what is there beyond such tasks? The world of computer vision and graphics, where mathematics stops being abstract: linear algebra and trigonometry bring facial expressions to life, and optimization turns into shaders on the GPU.

In the talk we will look at how face filters are arranged under the hood: from face tracking and segmentation through Core ML to realistic filters.

We will look at two approaches:

Based on face landmarks with point-by-point processing of different face zones.
Based on segmentation models with color processing of different segments.
We will pay special attention to the technical implementation: how Vision works, how segmentation Core ML models are arranged, and how to write your own shaders for CIKernel to achieve maximum performance.

title: KMP at X5 Tech: how big tech moves to new technologies
speakers: Anna Zharkova, Pavel Baranov, Yuri Grishin, Dmitry Alekseenkov, Denis Alexandrov
abstract:
If you still have doubts about KMP and want to learn more about cross-platform development — come to the round table. A team of tech leads from X5 will tell how they use KMP for the development of B2B and B2C applications. Anna Zharkova from Usetech Group will share examples she described in the book "Kotlin Multiplatform in Practice." We will discuss the secrets of migration, typical mistakes, and writing projects from scratch.

After the round table Anna will sign books for everyone who wants one.

title: Implementing Flutter CLI for Aurora OS
abstract:
For a cross-platform framework, a high-quality CLI implementation is critically important. Flutter has a ready-made solution — Flutter CLI, a framework component that allows developers to interact with Flutter SDK functions. To support platform-specific functions — such as building and installing applications, working with devices — Flutter uses the CLI and the API of a specific platform. In the talk I will tell you about the organization and implementation of the main Flutter CLI functions for supporting a new platform and how to extend the functionality of Flutter CLI for your own needs. I will give examples of the implementation for Aurora OS.

Useful for Flutter developers, developers of applications for Aurora OS, and framework developers.

Technologies used: Flutter, Dart, Flutter CLI, Aurora SDK.

title: Revealing the secrets: which design system tools will help everyone
abstract:
A design system significantly increases the efficiency of a development team. It provides a solid foundation of rules and standards, thanks to which developers can quickly make decisions and shorten the time to ship new features.

In the process of creating our design system we faced a number of difficulties. The standard tools turned out to be insufficient for solving certain problems, and we decided to develop our own — which we want to share with you. These tools simplify work with the design system, make the process of testing interfaces faster and more convenient, and as a bonus allow us to present our system to other teams in a great way.

title: Between offline and online: the magic of a consistent cache
abstract:
There are applications in which users need access to data even without a network. For this, complex caching schemes based on SQLite are often used. But the real problems begin when the application returns to online. Can you trust the saved data? And if not — which exactly do you need to update, and which to leave alone?

In the talk we will look at how to store and refresh data between sessions. We will look at real cases, compare approaches to synchronization, and honestly discuss their pros and cons.

title: SwiftUI: Scroll, Text, and a bit of magic
abstract:
"Community, or the Society of Entrepreneurs" is a small startup inside the big SberBusiness project.

I will tell you what interesting and non-standard tasks we faced and how we solved them in SwiftUI.

There will be:

relations with Scroll;
an original use of ContentShape;
what you need to know about Text when building a social app and not only that. How markdown, modifiers, and AttributedString get along inside one text. What to expect from TextRenderer and whether it will make our life easier.
You are in for not just an interesting technical talk but also useful code examples from the talk on GitHub.

title: Will you actually need this after the interview?
abstract: Sometimes candidates wonder: why ask in interviews about things that will never be needed on a project? We will discuss several cases when the technical knowledge tested at an interview can come in handy when developing applications, sometimes in the most unexpected ways.

title: We sawed up a monolith. Many of us, but it is one
abstract: I will tell you about the evolution of Yandex Smena. How we first hastily built a monolith, and then through pain and tears gained experience and moved away from the monolith toward a distributed architecture.

title: How we accelerate our smart devices
abstract:
When user scenarios touch several processes at once and voice interaction is an inseparable part of the scenario — how do you provide the user with the best experience? I will share how we at Yandex went through the path of improvements in the area of interaction speed. I will tell:

How the specifics of smart devices' work influenced our methods.
How we build and validate end-to-end metrics in order to always be aware of performance dynamics.
Which approaches we use to analyze slow user sessions and how Perfetto helps to identify bottlenecks.
I will show examples of improvements to the launch scenario of the Home screen and voice scenarios.

title: Online caller ID: secrets of the Live Caller ID implementation for iOS
abstract:
An overview of the implementation of the Live Caller ID feature on the iOS platform. This feature allows you to identify and block unwanted calls in real time.

We will look at the technical aspects of integrating the feature into a mobile application and at how the technology is arranged "under the hood," including the use of proxy servers on Apple's side to hide users' IP addresses, anonymous authentication, and homomorphic encryption combined with the private information retrieval protocol to ensure data confidentiality. We will touch on the interaction with Apple and the specifics of working with their API and compare with analogous capabilities on Android.

The talk will be of interest to mobile application developers specializing in security questions, client-server interaction, as well as developers of automatic anti-spam solutions.

title: When structs in Swift aren't your bro
abstract: I will tell you what a link map is and how to use it to find code that increases the application size the most. I will demonstrate features of the Swift compiler and situations when, for ordinary structs, dozens of megabytes of binary code are generated. I will show how we fought this problem in the Alfa-Bank iOS application, how many lines of code had to be rewritten, and by how many megabytes the application shrank. Spoiler: it was worth it!

title: Problems of mobile application gamification
abstract: How do you embed a full-fledged mini-game into a React Native application without being a game developer? In the talk — the real experience of a team that implemented a game mechanic inside a live fintech application, tested three approaches — Unity, React Native Game Engine, Godot — and went through CI/CD hell, an 800 MB weight, the absence of simulator support, and "patches with love." We did not make it to production with Unity, but we walked a path that may now save you weeks. And maybe even talk you out of a decision you would later regret.

title: Substituting dependencies without leaving traces
abstract:
Our project is a multi-repo where individual repositories are not only core components but also actively changing features. Quite often we face the task of integrating changes into the final application, and during the development of the integration we want to have access to not yet published functionality. Local snapshots help us with this, but they are not always convenient.

In the talk I will look at an alternative variant of delivering not yet published functionality into the final project, given to us by Gradle. We will also look at how, with the help of Gradle, we manage to substitute the necessary dependencies with local projects without any changes in the final one.

In the process we will see how, thanks to this approach, we learned to combine projects in one IDE in order to work with them as one whole.

title: How much does BDUI cost?
abstract:
If you are thinking about adopting a BDUI framework to speed up development processes, our experience may be useful to you. We at Alfa-Bank have been creating and developing our BDUI ecosystem since 2020.

Our talk will be devoted to the architecture of this solution, an overview of all the key components and the connections between them — from the design system and the shared repository to screenshot tests and a Kotlin DSL — as well as the challenges we had to overcome.

In the conclusion we will look at the available open source alternatives and reflect on whether in 2025 it is worth developing your own framework or it's better to use ready-made solutions.

title: (Non-)obvious connections: visualizing business logic through code
abstract:
As a mobile project grows, a moment inevitably comes when the complexity of the business logic reaches a level at which even experienced developers cannot keep the entire interaction picture in their heads. This leads to slow onboarding of newcomers, who take a long time to figure out the connections between components, as well as to difficult refactoring, in the course of which non-obvious dependencies break half of the application.

In the talk I will share our experience of creating a tool that automatically transforms code connections into an interactive graph. I will show how Kotlin Embedded Compiler Plugin, PSI tree analysis, and integration with Android Studio allowed us to create living documentation that never gets outdated and gave the ability to see the architecture through the eyes of the compiler — with all the connections and dependencies.

title: A non-obvious solution to an obvious problem: uploading files
abstract:
How a simple business task of supporting parallel file upload turned into deep research and a thoroughly worked-out solution. Instead of an old Java uploader on OkHttp, we managed to implement a lightweight and flexible coroutine-first uploader on top of java.nio and our own TLS wrapper.

I will also show how to minimize unnecessary allocations and achieve maximally stable operation on weak networks, even when uploading 4GB files.

title: Protecting Android application resources with Runtime Resource Overlay
abstract:
I will tell you about a little-known but powerful Android operating system mechanism — Runtime Resource Overlay (RRO). It allows an application to modify its own resources and the resources of other applications on the fly, without the need for recompilation.

RRO has great potential for protecting application resources. For example, you can store data in encrypted form and decrypt them only when the application starts via overlaying the resources. Such dynamic substitution of resources significantly complicates reverse engineering of a mobile application.

I will demonstrate several interesting bugs in the operating system that allow you to forbid installing an application on certain devices, as well as remove an application without user interaction.

The talk will be useful for developers of Android applications and mobile OSes, security researchers, and everyone interested in OS security and non-standard approaches to protecting mobile applications.

title: Why and how you should contribute to open source
abstract:
Open source moves the industry and your career. Using real examples I will show why contributing is not scary and not "for the chosen ones," but personally beneficial to you.

We will look at the fast path from "I want to help" to the first accepted PR: how to choose a project, where to look for issues, what forms of contribution besides code exist, what is important to know about repository rules and reviews. We will talk a little about Git/GitHub practices, touch on the mobile part including Flutter, and put together a checklist with which you will be able to make your first contribution that very evening.

title: Privacy-First AI on iOS: Building On-Device LLM Experiences with Swift + Core ML
abstract:
Privacy issues with cloud-based LLMs like ChatGPT are becoming more prevalent as AI gets more pervasive. A strong substitute is provided by Apple's on-device AI approach, which is fueled by Core ML and Swift. In order to avoid cloud fees, latency, and data privacy concerns, this talk delves deeply into running large language models (LLMs) only on iOS devices.

What we'll cover:

Why on-device LLMs are important: offline functionality, cost savings, and privacy advantages.
Core ML Optimization: how to compress and convert Hugging Face models like Mistral and Phi-3 for iOS, including Metal GPU acceleration and 4-bit/8-bit quantization.
Swift integration: developing a responsive SwiftUI chatbot that manages memory, generates text in real time, and uses local LLM inference.
Challenges and solutions: using Apple's MLX framework, fallbacks for older devices, and tradeoffs between model size and speed.
The talk includes a live demo of a fully offline ChatGPT-like app running on an iPhone, plus benchmarks across devices. You will leave with actionable steps to implement private, efficient AI in your apps—no server required. Key takeaways:

Practical steps to deploy LLMs on iOS using Core ML + Swift.
Techniques to balance model size, speed, and accuracy.
Future-proofing apps for Apple's on-device AI roadmap.
Target audience: intermediate/advanced iOS developers interested in AI, privacy, or cutting-edge Swift capabilities.

title: Live picture: animations and effects in a cross-platform BDUI engine
abstract:
I will look at how to breathe life into interfaces with the help of animations and effects in a cross-platform BDUI engine. You will learn about the difficulties of creating a universal API that works on Android and iOS, and about how to overcome the technical challenges. We will discuss:

1. The mechanism of flipping a picture: how to implement the effect of rotating an image and other view elements while keeping a single approach for both platforms.

2. A timer in the interface: the subtleties of embedding timers that synchronize and look the same on Android and iOS.

3. Animations in BDUI: the process of integrating smooth and expressive animations that make the interface interactive and attractive.

4. The blur effect: the secrets of creating a stylish blur that not only pleases the eye but also works efficiently on different devices.

title: Developing widgets on Android: what can go wrong
abstract:
At interviews you are often asked: "Which feature you implemented are you most proud of?" I often kindly envied candidates when they told what cool things they had had a chance to work on. Last year such a tasty problem appeared in my own resume too: I developed a desktop widget for our application.

We will discuss what nuances and difficulties you can encounter when developing widgets and why such tasks are important for professional growth.

title: Your first KMP application for Aurora OS
abstract:
We will talk about the prospects of adapting the Kotlin Multiplatform framework to Aurora OS and about what creating a KMP application looks like from a mobile developer's point of view.

We will talk about the QtBindings tool, which allows binding business logic on KMP with a graphical interface on Qt/QML. I will show how to use the Aurora OS API on the Kotlin side. As a bonus I will tell you about the current status of Compose Multiplatform support and how today you can already write applications on CMP for Aurora OS.

The talk will be useful for those who want to develop applications for Aurora OS while keeping the familiar technology stack and programming language, as well as for those who plan to port existing KMP applications to this operating system.

title: Java-Swift interop vs Kotlin-Swift export. The road to singularity
abstract:
JetBrains has announced the release of full direct export from Kotlin/Native to Swift by autumn 2025. This solution will help speed up the compilation of cross-platform libraries for iOS and will also solve a number of problems with performance and memory management.

This is the solution from the Kotlin side. But did you know that solutions for using Swift on Android have existed for a long time? It's both support for working with the Swift standard library (stdlib) and working with code through C/C++ and JNI. In 2025 Apple prepared a powerful tool, SwiftJava, which will allow converting code between languages and reusing the codebase of another technology, making code shared but without using KMP.

Which solution is better and when should you use one or the other? In the talk I will tell you about the differences between the work of the new tool for converting code from Java to Swift and back and the direct Kotlin-Swift export. We will talk about how these solutions can be used in practice, how and when to use them in Android and iOS applications including via Gradle and SPM, and what is better: native or cross-platform. We will discuss the pros, cons, limitations, and best practices.

title: From personal productivity to team productivity: the power of templating in the IDE
abstract:
One of the key problems in development remains the large amount of routine, boilerplate code that slows down work and increases the probability of errors. In the talk we will look at how a systematic approach to creating and using templates in the IDE allows to fundamentally solve this problem, and how creating a shared base of templates will help to simultaneously achieve several goals: significantly accelerate development, standardize processes in the team, and simplify the onboarding of new employees.

We will examine this approach in detail using the practical example of organizing the presentation layer and UDF architecture. You will learn about practices for creating and structuring templates, ways to store and synchronize them within a team, and also how to better "sell" technical decisions to your team.

The talk will be especially useful for developers who strive to minimize their routine, and for tech leads interested in increasing the overall efficiency of their teams.

title: The path of the indie hacker
abstract:
The indie topic is almost not discussed among us. Only a few developers, after work, find an interest in working on their own projects. Even fewer are those who decide to bring it to users and upload the project to the App Store. And only a handful earn money from it. I will tell you about my experience and share some best practices for a quick start.

In the talk there will be a lot of everything: ASO, the perfect onboarding, paywall, ASA, TikTok, and many stories of apps that did and did not make it to the App Store.

title: The day the API key set sail: mobile application attack scenarios through a hacker's eyes
abstract:
Why is it worth thinking about mobile application security as early as the development stage? In the talk I propose to look at a mobile application the way an attacker does: we will examine where and how an application can be attacked, what can be extracted from it, and which mistakes can cost a developer dearly.

This will be done with examples of real vulnerabilities from practice. We will look at the attack surface of mobile applications — I will tell you in detail about:

0-click attacks, which do not require user interaction;
1-click attacks, in which the user interacts with a malicious link or component;
other vectors — malware, attacks with physical access.
We will look at examples of real vulnerabilities that can be discovered in an application. We will talk about what they can lead to and how you can defend against them.

It will be of interest to mobile developers, security engineers, and architects who need to understand how their decisions can be used against them.

Technologies: Kotlin/Java, Android SDK, ProGuard, WebView, adb and Frida, mitmproxy.

title: One code style for all: how to tame SwiftLint at company scale
abstract:
Each team configures SwiftLint in its own way, and over time, instead of a single standard, you get dozens of fragmented configs. Some projects have one set of rules, others have completely different ones. As a result, code review boils down to endless discussions about style, new developers get lost in local quirks, and corporate standards remain only on paper.

In the talk I will tell you how to bring order with the help of Remote Configs in SwiftLint. I built a system in which rules are managed centrally, but at the same time teams retain the ability to flexibly extend them for their own needs. At the heart — a single repository with configurations in GitLab.

I will show the real architecture, integration examples, and the pitfalls worth taking into account. You will learn how to move from the chaos of fragmented lint rules to a transparent hierarchy: corporate → divisions → teams → projects.

The result — a single code style across the entire company, fewer disputes at code review, faster onboarding, and more predictable development quality.

title: Design—code. On the way to an ecosystem
abstract:
Today digital products grow faster than ever, and the more services there are, the harder it is to maintain a unified user experience. At Finam we have walked the path from separate products with disjoint UI to an attempt to build a unified ecosystem.

01. Design

We will figure out why fragmentation arises at the level of interfaces, explain how a design system becomes a tool for aligning the visual and product layer. We will briefly walk through its architecture: palette, tokens, themes, and the principles that set a single language for all teams.

02. Development

Then we will move on to how design is passed into code. We will show how we use the Figma API, generation of Dart tokens, and the Flutter architecture to reduce divergence between mockups and the product.

03. Strategy

In the conclusion we will discuss how to measure progress. We will look at types of consistency and at the maturity model that helps to assess the state of processes, understand the growth zone, and build the ecosystem's development as a long-term strategy rather than a set of point improvements.

The talk will be useful for everyone who works at the intersection of design and development and is looking for a practical path to an ecosystem in which decisions are synchronized from the mockup to production code.

title: Biohacking: can you make the body do what it doesn't want to?
abstract:
Want to sleep four hours, run marathons, not age, and keep your head clear until late at night? Biohacking promises all of this — and even more. But what of it really works, and what is a myth mixed on anxiety, marketing, and distrust of doctors?

Alexey Vodovozov — a doctor, medical journalist, author of the book "The Reasonable Patient" and the blog "Military Doctor's Examination Room" — proposes to put aside the supplements, switch off the podcast with a former IT guy turned biohacker, and look at scientific data.

Where does autosuggestion end and physiology begin? Why are test results not an instruction for action? And can you in general "hack" the body without breaking it along the way?

title: Closing of the Mobius 2025 Autumn conference
abstract: We sum up the results, recall bright moments, and tell about further plans. Come to the hall or join the broadcast so as not to miss anything!
